chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper explicitly provides a working link to the public GitHub repository in Section X (Conclusion): 'https://github.com/TUM-DSE/Treaty'.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: Section VIII-A states that experiments were performed on a 'real hardware testbed' consisting of 6 server machines with Intel Core i9-9900K CPUs and SGX enclaves, connected via a physical network switch.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: Section III ('THREAT AND FAULT MODEL') contains a dedicated section that formally defines the adversary capabilities (control over software stack outside enclave, view/modify non-enclave memory), specific failure assumptions (crash-fail recovery model), and explicitly states what is NOT protected against (side-channel attacks).
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: Section VIII provides rigorous, data-driven analysis of overheads. It quantifies throughput slowdown factors (e.g., 6x-15x for distributed Txs, 2x-5x for single-node), measures latency impacts, and evaluates specific costs like encryption overhead (up to 1.4x) and recovery protocol costs (1.5x-2x slowdown).
chain_of_thought_evaluation.major_weakness: The paper does not have a major weakness as it scores maximum points on all four criteria; however, if forced to identify an area for improvement, the fault model could be expanded to include Byzantine behavior beyond simple crashes.
